title: Moose Jaw Police Service introduces a New Opportunity for Students: ViPER Safety Patrol
content:
The Moose Jaw Police Service is excited to introduce a new opportunity for local students beginning or continuing post‑secondary studies this fall, who are interested in exploring a future in law enforcement and public safety.
This summer, we are launching the
ViPER (Visible Proactive Engagement Response) Safety Patrol
— a community‑focused initiative designed to give young people meaningful experience while helping keep Moose Jaw safe.
The ViPER program builds on a proven model developed by Chief Davis, who previously established
The Watch
in Lethbridge, Alberta — now celebrating its seventh year — as well as a Park Patrol program in Dryden, Ontario. These initiatives were inspired by innovative best practices he observed in Winnipeg, Vancouver, and Queensland, Australia.
“The ViPER is an opportunity for those considering a career in public safety to test the waters to see what aspects of public safety interest them.” Chief Davis said. “Ideally, we would like the ViPER to be a feeder league to a career in policing. But in my experience, some may realize that EMS, fire fighting, or social services appeals more to them – which is still a positive experience as it helps students select a career path and get exposure.”
As part of their role, ViPER team members will serve as additional eyes and ears for the Moose Jaw Police Service. They will support community safety initiatives such as Safe Walk, Visible Valuables, and bicycle safety programs. They will also assist with parking education and enforcement, and act as friendly ambassadors in Moose Jaw’s downtown.
Students interested in joining the ViPER Safety Patrol can apply online through the Moose Jaw Police Service website. Full program details and how to apply can be found on our website at
www.mjpolice.ca
.
We’re proud to offer this opportunity to local youth and look forward to the positive impact the ViPER Safety Patrol will have on our community.

title: STARS Lottery Scam
content:
To protect yourself, always keep these points in mind:
You will never be asked to send money to claim any lottery prize. If you are being asked to send a cheque, money order, or payment of any kind to claim your lottery prize, it is a scam. Hang up the phone or delete the email immediately. If you become suspicious, check with STARS directly to verify if you are a winner and if the lottery is legitimate.
In some instances, the fraudster has demanded payment via gift cards as a way to claim the prize. As a reminder, do not provide the scammer with the access codes for the gift cards under any circumstances.
The Moose Jaw Police Service would also like to remind the public not to provide personal information such as their Social Insurance Number (SIN), or banking details over the phone.
If you or someone you know has fallen victim to a scam, please get in touch with the Moose Jaw Police Service at 306-694-7600. If you prefer to remain anonymous, call the Moose Jaw Crime Stoppers with your information at 1-800-222-8477, go online at
p3tips.com, or download the mobile app.
For more information on fraud attempts, visit
Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre (antifraudcentre-centreantifraude.ca)

title: Can You Identify – Mischief Incident – UPDATE
content:
UPDATE:
The individual pictured has been identified.
————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————-
File # 2025-18376
The Moose Jaw Police Service is asking for the public’s assistance in identifying a male in connection with a mischief incident. On November 12, 2025, police responded to a report of an individual spray-painting or “tagging” a building on the 900 block of Home Street West. With video surveillance, police identified a young male in the area at the time of the incident.
The male was wearing all-black attire, with a white hat, and was carrying a skateboard. The male is a person of interest in an ongoing spray-painting incident.
Anyone with further information on the male suspect can contact CRIME STOPPERS at 1-800-222-8477 or the Moose Jaw Police Service at (306) 694-7600
